value.market_read: WTI Curve: WTI settled at $70.10 in the prior session, extending gains tied to the June 24 WPSR covering the week ending June 19. The reported crude draw and tight Cushing balances lifted the prompt and reinforced the front of the curve into peak summer demand. The curve remains firmly backwardated, with the M1–M12 spread at $3.33 (August 2026 at $70.10 vs July 2027 at $66.77), signaling the market is pricing genuine near-term supply tightness rather than a storage surplus. The M1–M2 spread of roughly 29 cents ($70.10 vs $69.81) confirms a firm prompt without stress-level dislocation. Watch: Watch Cushing draws in the next WPSR — continued stock declines would steepen prompt backwardation, while a build would flatten the front. Brent Curve: Brent settled at $73.44, holding a $3.34 premium to WTI after the same WPSR-driven prompt firmness rippled across the Atlantic basin. The Brent curve shows a slight front-end kink — September at $73.51 edges above the August front — before sliding into backwardation deeper out, with M1–M12 at $2.25. That shallower backwardation versus WTI reflects a global barrel that is tight but less acutely so at the prompt than domestic Cushing-linked crude. The near-flat M1–M2 structure signals balanced prompt supply internationally even as the back end drifts lower. Watch: Watch OPEC+ output signaling and Hormuz risk premium, either of which could re-steepen the Brent front. RBOB Gasoline Curve: RBOB settled at $2.9102, the strongest point on its curve as summer driving demand pulls hard on the prompt. The steep backwardation from August through winter — M1–M12 at $0.5315 — reflects the seasonal premium for summer-spec gasoline that unwinds sharply into the fall RVP transition, with the curve bottoming around February 2027 near $2.19 before the spring rebound to the 2027 summer strip. This front-loaded shape is textbook driving-season pricing, and the strong prompt supports gasoline cracks against a WTI that is itself firm. Watch: Watch the September roll and the summer-to-winter grade transition, which typically deflates the prompt premium quickly. ULSD Diesel Curve: ULSD settled at $3.2353, holding a firm prompt with the steepest sustained backwardation of the complex, M1–M12 at $0.5327. Unlike gasoline, the distillate curve descends smoothly with no seasonal trough, indicating persistent structural tightness in middle distillates rather than a purely seasonal pull. The consistent month-over-month declines through July 2027 signal the market expects prompt diesel tightness to ease only gradually. This underpins healthy distillate cracks and supports refinery incentives to maximize diesel yield. Watch: Watch distillate inventory trajectory into late summer, as tight balances leave little cushion ahead of the fall harvest and heating build.
value.kpi_wti: WTI settled at $70.10 for the August front month, firm on WPSR-driven prompt tightness and Cushing draws.
value.kpi_brent: Brent settled at $73.44, holding a modest premium to WTI with a shallow front-end kink into September.
value.kpi_rbob: RBOB settled at $2.9102, the peak of its curve as summer driving demand front-loads the gasoline complex.
value.kpi_ulsd: ULSD settled at $3.2353, backwardated smoothly through 2027 on persistent structural distillate tightness.
value.kpi_bw: The Brent–WTI spread at $3.34 is relatively narrow, weakening US export arb economics and keeping more barrels domestic — a re-widening would require WTI to ease relative to Brent.
value.kpi_curve_regime: Both crude curves are backwardated (WTI steeper at $3.33 M1–M12 vs Brent's $2.25), signaling near-term supply tightness rather than a storage-surplus contango play.
